summary: Rain-soaked RFK delivers epic Victim→Foolish Heart→Dark Star and soaring set
blurb: The night shines with a monster Victim Or The Crime > Foolish Heart > Dark Star suite that erupts into a transcendent Dark Star and rolls straight into Music Never Stopped. Brent Mydland’s keyboards are in peak form while Jerry’s guitar soars throughout the show. Recording quality varies, but the top‑tier soundboard capture preserves the storm‑driven energy.
review:
July 12, 1990 at RFK Stadium was a rain‑baptized triumph that has become one of the most talked‑about shows of the late‑period Dead. From the opening “Let The Good Times Roll” through a fiery first set, the band built momentum that exploded in the second set’s centerpiece: Victim Or The Crime into Foolish Heart and a monster Dark Star that stretched past 30 minutes, eventually spilling into an electrifying Music Never Stopped. Brent Mydland’s Hammond B3 textures and ad‑libs were among his last great outings, while Jerry Garcia delivered soaring leads on “Feel Like A Stranger” and a blistering solo that lifted the whole band. Phil Lesh’s thick, supportive bass underpinned every jam, and despite a few mix quirks the dual‑drum assault from Mickey Hart and Bill Kreutzmann drove the space segment into stadium‑shaking heights. The rain added an undeniable ambience, especially on the encore “Touch of Grey” and the heartfelt Hey Jude finale, cementing this night as a must‑listen snapshot of the Dead’s 1990 tour.

For listeners seeking the definitive audio experience, the soundboard‑matrix recording (gd90-07-12.sbd.mcatee.2582.sbeok.shnf) offers crystal‑clear instrument separation, just enough crowd bleed to feel the storm, and no drop‑outs – it captures both the musical fireworks and the wet‑night atmosphere with remarkable fidelity.

rating_calculation.sentiment_rationale: All ten recorded analyses carry a positive sentiment, repeatedly praising the Victim>Foolish Heart>Dark Star suite, Brent's keyboards, Jerry's guitar work and overall show energy. Multiple reviewers use superlatives such as "legendary", "transcendent" and "stellar", indicating strong consensus of high quality.

best_recording.reason: Excellent SBD‑matrix mix captures the rain ambience, clear balance of instruments and full band dynamics with no drop‑outs, making it the most faithful representation of this legendary night.

key_highlights: Monster Victim Or The Crime > Foolish Heart > Dark Star suite that rolls into Music Never Stopped
Brent Mydland’s Hammond B3 textures and vocal ad‑libs in top form
Rain‑soaked stadium ambience adds intensity to the second set jams
Jerry Garcia’s soaring guitar solos on Feel Like A Stranger and Dark Star

band_performance.Jerry: Delivers soaring leads and incendiary solos, especially on Feel Like A Stranger, Dark Star and the Victim>Foolish Heart>Dark Star suite; vocals are strong throughout.
band_performance.Phil: Provides thick, supportive bass that drives the high‑energy jams; several reviewers note his playing as a highlight of the night.
band_performance.Brent: Shows up with rich Hammond B3 textures, quirky synth moments and vocal ad‑libs; praised as one of his final great performances before his hiatus.
band_performance.Mickey: Drum sound is powerful and propels the space segment, though a few listeners mention mix issues in the drum channel.
